legacy_behavior: ScheduleManager creates the main tasklet, tracks current/previous tasklets, maintains runnable queue links, inserts at tail or after current, removes by repairing links, and reports run count as queued tasklets plus main.
rust_state: carbon-scheduler-core exists and the scheduler fixture gate covers stable main/current identity, FIFO runnable order, insert/remove, switch counts, blocked/paused/alive/scheduled state, run-count assertions, and deterministic trace events for the current core slice.
gap: No base scheduler state-machine gap remains for the covered core slice; broader reschedule, switch-trap, timeout, and FFI identity behavior are tracked in separate findings.
recommended_task: Keep this base state model in the scheduler fixture gate and work remaining scheduler parity through the narrower open findings.
performance_metric: tasklet switches/sec; queue insert/remove ops/sec

legacy_behavior: The scheduler core should own tasklet/channel lifecycle state, blocked sender/receiver queues, balance, preference, close/open/clear, and block-trap mutation rules rather than leaving those decisions in Python wrapper objects.
rust_state: carbon-scheduler-core exposes an owner-thread CoreScheduler API with CoreTaskletId/CoreChannelId handles and tests for unbuffered channel rendezvous, positive/negative balance signs, blocked queue fronts, preference clamping, close/open/clear, remove-blocked cleanup, block-trap no-mutation checks, peer-immediate handoff preference, and tasklet runtime snapshots. carbon-scheduler-python live tasklet/channel objects now carry opaque CoreTaskletId/CoreChannelId handles, mirror unbuffered channel balance/preference/block-trap/blocked-queue transitions through CoreScheduler, consume CoreChannelOperationResult sender/receiver IDs plus balance and immediate peer handoff for covered unbuffered send/receive transfer decisions, use CoreScheduler snapshots for sender-preferred pre-receive scheduling probes, use CoreScheduler queue-front results for channel.queue introspection, keep current-thread runnable PyObject registries in thread-local compatibility storage while CoreScheduler remains runnable ordering authority, mirror tasklet alive/scheduled/paused/times_switched_to through CoreScheduler snapshots for covered setup/run/finish/block/continuation/clear/kill paths, and expose public tasklet lifecycle/scheduling/block-trap/switch-count properties, public channel preference/closing/closed properties, plus the C API times-switched getter from CoreScheduler snapshots while the existing Python/C API tests pass.
gap: The PyO3 bridge still stores Python payload/exception transfer behavior, pending payload close-state details, broader PyObject queue identity adapters, Greenlet continuation state, broader run-queue/scheduling decisions, pending exception/kill/continuation details, callbacks, and refcount/GC cleanup; core_ownership_status remains partial.
recommended_task: Make CoreScheduler tasklet/channel snapshots authoritative for broader run-queue decisions, Python payload handoff tokens, queue identity adapters, callbacks, and lifecycle transitions while keeping only Python callables/exceptions/refcount glue in the bridge, then rerun unchanged Python/C API tests, in-process C API probes, scheduler fixtures, and IO Scheduler.h smoke.
performance_metric: channel ops/sec; bridge overhead; p95 channel wake latency

legacy_behavior: Schedule records BACK/FRONT_PLUS_ONE reschedule or tags current for removal; targeted runs stop at the original end tasklet so newly created tasklets are not executed in that loop.
rust_state: Current fixtures cover FIFO, targeted-run ordering, nested schedule ordering, schedule_remove pause/reinsert semantics, and switch-trap schedule/schedule_remove no-mutation rejection through trace expectations.
gap: Rust parity would still miss FRONT_PLUS_ONE, broader BACK reschedule edge cases, and some targeted-run boundary behavior.
recommended_task: Implement the remaining RescheduleType-equivalent state and add BACK, FRONT_PLUS_ONE, and targeted-run boundary fixtures.
performance_metric: reschedule ops/sec

legacy_behavior: When main blocks, the scheduler either errors immediately if no other tasklets are runnable or drains runnable children before raising deadlock if main is still blocked.
rust_state: The scheduler fixture corpus covers all four legacy main-tasklet deadlock cases: immediate receive deadlock, immediate send deadlock, receive-after-runnable-child-drain deadlock, and send-after-runnable-child-drain deadlock. The after-child fixtures assert FIFO child run_order 0..9 before the RuntimeError Deadlock event; all four fixtures assert run_count=1, main remains unblocked, and channel balance/blocked queues return to zero. The scheduler fixture gate passes 55/55 with ordered events and invariant checks.
gap: No pure-core main-tasklet deadlock fixture gap remains for the legacy test_channel.py cases; exact Python RuntimeError object/text behavior remains covered through the bridge/unchanged-suite evidence.
recommended_task: Keep deadlock.json, main_receive_immediate_deadlock.json, main_send_immediate_deadlock.json, and main_send_deadlock_after_running_child_tasklets.json in the scheduler fixture gate while broader Python exception behavior stays in the bridge gate.

legacy_behavior: Nested tasklet yielding switches to parent tasklets and climbs blocked parent chains; Run assigns parent links and guards cyclic re-entry.
rust_state: Nested Greenlet behavior is deferred in docs, but targeted run order depends on it.
gap: The core boundary does not yet define parent links and nested scheduling independent of Greenlet.
recommended_task: Model parent TaskletId links and nested scheduling in core state; leave actual Greenlet switching to FFI.
performance_metric: nested switch depth; parent-chain traversal cost

legacy_behavior: RunNTasklets is count-limited; RunTaskletsForTime is monotonic-time-limited, always lets the first timeout check pass, resets mode state, and exposes completed/switched counters.
rust_state: Count-limited run support is implemented in the core trace runner. scheduler_run_n_tasklets_one_fifo_boundary proves one-tasklet FIFO boundaries; four scheduler_run_n_tasklets_limited_*schedule_order*.json fixtures prove repeated run_n_tasklets(1) schedule-order pumping for nested and non-nested single-level and multi-level scenarios with ordered trace expectations and exact limit=1 run-start counts. Timeout fixtures cover zero-timeout first-tasklet progress, remaining scheduled tasklets after an expired timeout, a nested timeout remainder, and simple completed/switched timeout counters. The Python/C API bridge also passes the real legacy Scheduler.cpp run-n and practical timeout source-slice tests.
gap: The pure core still models positive timeout_ns as a drain instead of a real monotonic-time deadline, so final RunTaskletsForTime policy parity is not complete in deterministic fixtures.
recommended_task: Add a monotonic clock abstraction or deterministic timeout-step fixture mode that can prove positive-time deadline stop/resume behavior without wall-clock flakiness, while keeping the existing run_n_tasklets and timeout-counter fixtures green.
performance_metric: timeout overhead per switch; completed/switched counters

legacy_behavior: Every current-tasklet change calls OnSwitch and then global Python and fast C schedule callbacks with previous/next tasklets.
rust_state: Callback execution is deferred; trace callback points are not defined in fixtures.
gap: Later adapters need deterministic callback points and identity ordering.
recommended_task: Emit core switch/callback-point trace events with previous/next TaskletId; defer callable invocation to FFI.
performance_metric: callback-point overhead per switch

legacy_behavior: Switch trap is an integer level; any nonzero level blocks switching while getters expose exact level.
rust_state: CoreScheduler stores switch-trap state as a per-run-queue integer level, with unit coverage for independent queue levels. The scheduler fixture corpus covers trapped send, receive, raise_exception, kill, tasklet.run, tasklet.switch, schedule, and schedule_remove no-mutation behavior in scheduler_switch_trap_no_state_mutation with exact switch_trap_error counts and zero mutating scheduler.schedule/scheduler.schedule_remove events, plus counted nested levels in scheduler_switch_trap_nested_level, where level 2 rejects, level 1 still rejects, and level 0 allows the direct tasklet run to complete.
gap: No pure-core counted switch-trap level or trapped operation no-mutation fixture gap remains for the current semantic slice; Python exception object/text fidelity remains bridge-owned.
recommended_task: Keep scheduler_switch_trap_nested_level and scheduler_switch_trap_no_state_mutation in the fixture gate while further schedule/reschedule and Python exception behavior moves through separate findings.

legacy_behavior: Run handles completed, yielded, blocked, tasklet-exception, failed-switch, and requires-removal paths while cleaning parent, queue, alive, and timeout state.
rust_state: The core now models completed, blocked, and paused tasklet outcomes, including schedule_remove pause/reinsert and blocked-channel cleanup fixtures.
gap: Tasklet-exception, failed-switch, requires-removal, timeout-state, and broader parent cleanup paths remain unproven by pure-core fixtures.
recommended_task: Add fixtures for exception/removal cleanup paths, failed switch cleanup, and timeout-mode reset once the core owns those semantics.

legacy_behavior: ScheduleManager is per Python thread, uses a closing-manager map during destruction, tracks active manager count, and clears tasklets for finishing threads.
rust_state: Thread ownership and cleanup are deferred; no adapter contract exists.
gap: Rust core/FFI split lacks thread-local ownership and teardown rules.
recommended_task: Define core close/drain API and FFI thread-local adapter contract with cleanup tests.
performance_metric: active manager count; leaked tasklets/managers after teardown

legacy_behavior: Legacy uses intrusive links for O(1) queue operations and telemetry zones around scheduler run paths.
rust_state: Rust has scheduler fixture and benchmark plumbing, but no matched optimized legacy scheduler process baseline, no final-report-ready scheduler benchmark row, and no proof that current string/BTreeMap/VecDeque choices improve throughput or latency.
gap: Scheduler speedup and queue-choice claims remain not comparable until the legacy scheduler baseline and matched benchmark rows exist.
recommended_task: Add matched legacy/Rust scheduler benchmarks seeded from semantic workloads and emit dashboard JSON only after both implementations pass the same workload.
performance_metric: switch throughput; p50/p95/p99 switch latency; peak memory per tasklet

legacy_behavior: Tasklet owns lifecycle state: alive, scheduled, blocked, paused, first_run, reschedule, removal, kill_pending, thread manager, counters, args, and transfer state.
rust_state: Core fixtures cover tasklet new/run/complete, bind/setup/rebind, insert/remove, pause via schedule_remove, unbind paused, kill pending/immediate, blocked state, switch counts, and active/all-time counters for the current symbolic slice.
gap: First-run/reschedule edge cases, thread-manager ownership, Python args/kwargs/object lifetime, and exception transfer state remain in separate core/FFI findings.
recommended_task: Finish the remaining lifecycle edge fixtures and keep Python object lifetime/args behavior in the PyO3 bridge gate.

legacy_behavior: Tasklet::Run rejects dead/blocked, silently ignores wrong-thread run, and differs by nested-tasklet mode; Switch validates args, trap, thread, blocked/dead state, and increments times_switched_to.
rust_state: Targeted run fixtures cover t1/t2 nested and non-nested ordering; tasklet_lifecycle_remove_insert_switch_times covers direct switch success and times_switched_to; scheduler_switch_trap_no_state_mutation and scheduler_switch_trap_nested_level cover switch-trap rejection; tasklet_invalid_run_switch_no_mutation covers blocked/dead direct run/switch rejection without queue or channel mutation. The scheduler fixture gate passes 55/55.
gap: Wrong-thread run/switch, Python argument validation/object identity details, and FRONT_PLUS_ONE/reschedule behavior remain outside this pure-core fixture slice.
recommended_task: Keep the current tasklet run/switch fixtures green, then add wrong-thread and bridge-level Python object/API tests plus remaining reschedule fixtures under SCH-CORE-002.
performance_metric: tasklet switches/sec; targeted-run ordering; invalid-operation rejection cost

legacy_behavior: Tasklet stores blocked channel, blocked direction, and blocked-list links; channel cleanup and kill paths rely on this state.
rust_state: Fixtures only assert simple blocked state and balance.
gap: Balance-only modeling can miss stale queue membership and double-unblock bugs.
recommended_task: Make blocked membership a scheduler-owned invariant keyed by TaskletId and ChannelId with block/unblock/kill/clear tests.

legacy_behavior: Kill is idempotent for dead/already-pending, rejects wrong-thread kills, unblocks blocked tasklets, supports pending insertion, and restores blocked state on failed immediate delivery.
rust_state: TaskletExit Python injection is deferred, but no symbolic kill model exists.
gap: Core cannot preserve queue/alive/blocked/channel-balance behavior for kill independent of Python exceptions.
recommended_task: Model immediate and pending kill as core control events with blocked-channel cleanup and dead-tasklet no-op behavior.

legacy_behavior: ThrowException and transfer arguments carry exception state, pending/immediate delivery, TaskletExit special cases, and blocked-state restoration.
rust_state: Exact Python exception behavior is deferred and symbolic payloads are missing.
gap: Channel send_throw/send_exception and tasklet throw cannot be core-fixtured yet.
recommended_task: Add symbolic ErrorPayload and pending/immediate delivery state to the trace model; defer Python traceback/value conversion to FFI.

legacy_behavior: Tasklet tracks all-time/active counts, times_switched_to, start/end/run-time, and resets switch count on bind.
rust_state: Core fixtures now assert times_switched_to transitions, bind reset, active_tasklet_count, and all_time_tasklet_count for lifecycle, bind/setup/rebind, schedule_remove, kill, and paused/unbind cases.
gap: Start/end/run-time metrics and broader counter transitions under exception, timeout, and cross-thread paths remain outside pure-core coverage.
recommended_task: Add remaining metric/counter fixtures for exception, timeout, and cross-thread paths, and keep Python timing properties in the bridge gate.
performance_metric: times_switched_to; active/all-time tasklet count

legacy_behavior: Bind validates Python callability, captures method/module/file/line metadata, wraps dont_raise callables, and stores context-manager and exception-handler PyObjects.
rust_state: Core lane excludes Python objects; FFI metadata plan is not defined.
gap: Adapter parity for diagnostics and Python callback behavior can be lost.
recommended_task: Define core optional metadata fields while keeping callable validation, wrappers, context managers, and exception handlers in the Python bridge.

legacy_behavior: Channel is an unbuffered rendezvous primitive; send blocks without receiver, receive blocks without sender, balance is positive for senders and negative for receivers, opposite queues match FIFO.
rust_state: Core fixtures cover channel state, blocked sender/receiver queues, positive/negative balance accounting, FIFO transfer matching, and scheduler resume actions for the current unbuffered slice.
gap: No base unbuffered channel state-machine gap remains for the covered core slice; preference, close/open, exception, cleanup, and FFI details are tracked in narrower findings.
recommended_task: Keep blocking_send, blocking_receive, send_receive_match, and queue-order fixtures in the scheduler gate while closing the narrower channel findings.
performance_metric: channel ops/sec; p50/p99 transfer latency

legacy_behavior: Channel preference defaults to receiver -1; sender/receiver/neither preferences change continuation order after transfer.
rust_state: Receiver preference, sender preference, receiver-first sender preference, simple neutral preference, neutral tasklet transfer, multi-party neutral ordering, and main-side send/receive preference matrix fixtures pass.
gap: Pure-core preference ordering is covered for the current matrix; Python-visible tasklet identity, callback identity, and broader bridge behavior remain outside this fixture gate.
recommended_task: Keep channel preference fixtures in the scheduler gate and finish bridge/FFI identity assertions before promoting full scheduler parity.
performance_metric: switches per channel transfer by preference

legacy_behavior: close sets closing and transitions to closed only when balance reaches zero; open clears both; closed/closing reject queue growth but allow draining existing peers.
rust_state: Core fixtures cover close/open state, closed send/receive errors, and close cleanup for blocked senders and receivers.
gap: Broader rejected would-block cases, reopen-after-drain combinations, and Python iterator/exception surface remain outside pure core coverage.
recommended_task: Add remaining close/reopen edge fixtures and keep Python iterator/error text in the bridge gate.

legacy_behavior: Would-block under block_trap raises without externally visible balance/queue mutation; failed yield/schedule paths remove blocked tasklets and clean transfer state.
rust_state: Core fixtures cover would-block block_trap send/receive no-mutation and nonblocking main block_trap transfers against waiting peers.
gap: Failed yield/schedule cleanup and broader interrupted transfer cleanup remain unproven in pure core.
recommended_task: Add interrupted yield/schedule cleanup fixtures and keep Python exception cleanup in the bridge gate.

legacy_behavior: Send can transfer value or exception payload; Receive raises transferred exceptions, restoring captured tuples or setting exception objects with arguments.
rust_state: Core now has symbolic value, send_exception, and send_throw payload transfer coverage in channel_exception_payloads; exact Python traceback/object restoration is deferred to the bridge.
gap: Pure-core symbolic exception transfer is covered for current cases, but exact PyErr restoration, traceback/value identity, and broader TaskletExit cleanup remain FFI work.
recommended_task: Keep symbolic exception-transfer fixtures in the scheduler gate and add bridge tests for sys.exc_info, traceback, and exact Python error values.
performance_metric: exception transfer latency relative to value transfer

legacy_behavior: Blocked removal updates intrusive links, adjusts balance by blocked direction, clears direction, and is reused by kill, clear, yield failure, and pop paths.
rust_state: Core fixtures cover blocked sender cleanup, blocked receiver cleanup, clear blocked tasklets, and queue-front order for current symbolic cases.
gap: Pending kill after completed transfer, yield-failure cleanup, and broader clear/error edge cases remain open.
recommended_task: Add pending-kill completed-transfer and yield-failure cleanup fixtures, and expand clear/error edge cases.
performance_metric: blocked tasklet removal time under N blocked tasklets

legacy_behavior: BlockedQueueFront returns first blocked receiver if present, otherwise first blocked sender; sender/receiver queues are FIFO from the user-visible front.
rust_state: channel_queue_introspection_order covers ordered blocked sender/receiver queues and queue-front snapshots in the pure core fixture gate.
gap: Core queue-order evidence exists, but full PyChannel_GetQueue object identity and C API consumer behavior remain in the bridge gate.
recommended_task: Keep queue-order fixtures in the scheduler gate and add bridge/C API assertions for queue-head object identity.

legacy_behavior: Global channel callback fires on every send/receive attempt with channel, tasklet, is_sending, and will_block computed before mutation.

legacy_behavior: Channel tracks active instances globally and UnblockAllActiveChannels clears active channels with nonzero balance.

legacy_behavior: Plain scheduler.run FIFO creates three tasklets, run count 4 before run, run count 1 after run, completion order t1t2t3.

legacy_behavior: Nested scheduling tests create tasklets from tasklets, call scheduler.schedule, and assert mode-dependent completion strings, including yield-to-blocked channel wakeups.

gap: Python object/API parity remains outside the semantic fixture layer.

Callback invocation and callable lifetime are not implemented.

legacy_behavior: blocking_send and blocking_receive require targeted run to block tasklet, return to main, set run_count 1, keep tasklet alive/blocked, and expose balance +1/-1.
rust_state: blocking_send.json and blocking_receive.json are enforced by the scheduler fixture runner with ordered events and final state checks for tasklet alive/blocked/scheduled state, run count, and channel balance.
gap: No fixture-runner enforcement gap remains for these blocking cases.

legacy_behavior: Main tasklet block_trap=true still allows non-blocking send/receive against waiting peers and transfers original payload.

legacy_behavior: Main cannot block on send/receive as last runnable; if children are runnable they are drained before deadlock error.
rust_state: main_receive_immediate_deadlock and main_send_immediate_deadlock cover the last-runnable receive/send RuntimeError Deadlock cases without mutating channel balance or blocked queues. deadlock and main_send_deadlock_after_running_child_tasklets cover receive/send after ten runnable child tasklets drain in order before the deadlock event. The scheduler fixture gate passes 55/55 and all four deadlock fixtures pass with ordered event checks.
gap: No pure-core fixture gap remains for the four legacy main-tasklet deadlock cases; Python exception object identity and exact traceback behavior remain bridge-owned.

legacy_behavior: Preference 1 completes senders before receivers, preference -1 delays sender completion, and preference 0 resumes neither inline with precise deferred order.
rust_state: Receiver preference, sender preference, receiver-first sender preference, simple neutral preference, neutral tasklet-transfer, multi-party neutral ordering, and main-side preference matrix fixtures exist.

legacy_behavior: block_trap rejects would-block send/receive on main and worker tasklets without mutating balance.

legacy_behavior: Killing/pending-killing blocked tasklets removes them from channel balance; channel.clear kills blocked senders; pending kill after completed transfer prevents post-transfer code under both preferences.

legacy_behavior: Tasklet lifecycle tests cover run, args/kwargs, remove().run, remove().switch, schedule_remove paused state, kill, and times_switched_to.

A simple setup/run callable workload, initial safe C API tasklet/channel/scheduler smoke paths, an expanded C++ Scheduler.h tasklet lifecycle/run-control/channel-preference/invalid-argument/inside-tasklet-send smoke, and the 210-test unchanged legacy Python scheduler suite pass, including channel blocking/deadlock/preference/refcount/inter-thread/cleanup behavior, block-trap send/receive errors, closed/open basics, channel iterator smoke, block-trap balance no-mutation checks, and QueueChannel buffered wrapper behavior.

legacy_behavior: Resource groups import/export YAML/CSV, walk directories, compute checksums/compressed sizes, preserve binary operations/modes, and return legacy ResultType values.

legacy_behavior: Bundle create/unpack writes a bundle manifest, stores the embedded resource group and chunk payloads by checksum-derived CDN location, validates checksums, supports local raw chunks and remote-CDN compressed payloads, and reconstitutes resource bytes on unpack.
rust_state: Rust now matches current local create-bundle, remote-CDN compressed create-bundle, create-bundle zero-chunk and missing-resource-source failure cleanup, local unpack, compressed remote-CDN unpack, 42-chunk local unpack boundary evidence, missing-chunk unpack failure cleanup, remote-CDN mirror download/cache-hit stats through the CLI, and create/unpack bundle CLI fixture bytes for the covered corpus.
gap: Broader bundle corpus, failure behavior, and network-backed remote/catalog integration still block a full bundle parity claim.
recommended_task: Extend bundle byte-compatibility fixtures across destination types, malformed manifests, missing payloads, and CLI failure modes.

legacy_behavior: Patch groups wrap target resource groups, generate checksum-derived diff resource names, apply patches with temp files, chunks, checksums, and removed-resource metadata.

gap: Broader apply failure behavior, temp-file cleanup, additional malformed patch payload modes, and larger patch corpus parity still block a full patch parity claim.
recommended_task: Extend patch fixtures across additional malformed payloads, apply failure cleanup, temp-file behavior, and larger patch corpora before promoting patch parity.

legacy_behavior: ResourceTools computes MD5, FNV, rolling checksums, matching chunks, chunk indexes, file stream checksums, and platform-specific binary operations/path behavior.

legacy_behavior: Externally visible compressed resources and MD5 checksums must match legacy compatibility rules, including gzip member headers used by legacy fixture payloads.

source_pair: final HTML report requirements -> report readiness
legacy_behavior: Final report may claim gains only after matching parity and benchmark gates pass.
rust_state: Progress HTML/report-readiness gates exist and current scheduler/resources/benchmark evidence is substantial but still explicitly report_ready=false for scheduler fixtures, legacy scheduler, Rust scheduler Python, IO workloads, Rust resources, and Tier 1 benchmarks.

recommended_task: Keep final report gated until every feature/performance/architecture claim links to passing evidence and unresolved gaps stay visible in tasks.md.

legacy_behavior: QueueChannel tests cover buffered sends, balance/length, queued data, blocking receive wakeups, queued exceptions, main-tasklet errors, block_trap, nested order, and main receive draining.
rust_state: QueueChannel remains the legacy Python wrapper over the Rust-backed base channel, and all 10 unchanged legacy test_queuechannel.py tests pass against the Rust extension through the rust-scheduler-python gate.
gap: No bridge parity artifact gap remains for legacy QueueChannel behavior; a pure-core buffered-channel model is intentionally deferred unless a later migration decision moves QueueChannel out of Python.
recommended_task: Keep QueueChannel as the legacy Python wrapper for compatibility; add pure-core buffered-channel fixtures only if QueueChannel is later ported out of the Python adapter.

legacy_behavior: Sends with no receiver complete without blocking, increment data_queue-backed balance, expose queued payloads, and make len(channel) match queued item count.
rust_state: The legacy QueueChannel wrapper path passes unchanged tests for non-blocking send, balance, queued data, len(channel), and buffered receive against the Rust-backed base channel.
gap: No bridge gap remains for buffered sends/len/balance; no pure-core buffered-channel trace exists because QueueChannel is kept as the Python compatibility wrapper.
recommended_task: Keep the wrapper evidence in rust-scheduler-python; implement core BufferedChannel events only if the compatibility strategy changes.
performance_metric: buffered enqueue/dequeue ops/sec; p95 enqueue latency; allocation count

legacy_behavior: Empty QueueChannel receive blocks the receiver; later send wakes that receiver instead of buffering and leaves receiver unblocked with run count 2.
rust_state: The unchanged test_queuechannel.TestQueueChannels.test_blocking_receive case passes against the Rust extension, and the PyO3 bridge unit smoke explicitly checks direct send-to-blocked-receiver handoff with buffer length and balance returning to zero.
gap: No bridge gap remains for QueueChannel blocking receive wakeup; pure-core fixture coverage is deferred because QueueChannel remains a Python wrapper.
recommended_task: Keep this as bridge evidence unless QueueChannel is migrated into core later.
performance_metric: receiver wake latency; blocked queue mutation latency

legacy_behavior: QueueChannel queues exception payloads and raises ValueError on receive; send_throw accepts sys.exc_info and restores traceback behavior.
rust_state: The unchanged QueueChannel send_exception/send_throw tests pass against the Rust extension, and the PyO3 bridge unit smoke validates queued exception type, value, and traceback behavior through the legacy Python wrapper.
gap: No bridge gap remains for QueueChannel queued exceptions; pure-core symbolic QueueChannel error events are intentionally unnecessary while QueueChannel stays in Python.
recommended_task: Keep exact Python exception/traceback behavior in the bridge gate; add core events only if QueueChannel is migrated into core later.

legacy_behavior: Empty QueueChannel.receive on main raises RuntimeError; block_trap-protected receive raises on main and worker without leaking state.
rust_state: The unchanged QueueChannel main-tasklet empty receive and block_trap receive tests pass against the Rust extension in the 210-test Python suite.
gap: No bridge gap remains for these rejected receive cases; pure-core no-mutation fixtures are deferred with the rest of a possible future BufferedChannel port.
recommended_task: Keep this behavior covered through the unchanged legacy QueueChannel suite.
performance_metric: rejected receive ops/sec with zero state mutations

legacy_behavior: A blocked QueueChannel receiver that spawns nested tasklets around receives must produce taskletOrder ['a', 1, 2, 'b', 'c'].
rust_state: The unchanged test_queuechannel.TestQueueChannels.test_blocked_tasklets_greenlet_is_not_parent case passes against the Rust extension in the 210-test Python suite.
gap: No bridge gap remains for QueueChannel nested order; pure-core QueueChannel fixture coverage is deferred unless QueueChannel is ported out of Python.
recommended_task: Keep nested QueueChannel behavior in the bridge gate for now.
performance_metric: completed tasklets/sec; p95 wake-to-resume latency

legacy_behavior: QueueChannel is a Python subclass of _scheduler.channel, calls super().__init__(self), exposes data_queue, overrides balance/send/receive, and implements __len__.
rust_state: The PyO3 gate builds/imports the Rust extension, imports the real legacy scheduler package, constructs QueueChannel as a subclass of the Rust-backed channel, verifies preference=1, len/balance through data_queue, buffered value receive, queued exception/throw behavior, direct blocked-receiver handoff, and all 10 unchanged legacy QueueChannel tests.
gap: No QueueChannel bridge compatibility gap remains for the unchanged legacy suite; future pure-core migration would need separate fixtures.
recommended_task: Treat QueueChannel as covered by the Python bridge until a deliberate core-buffered-channel migration is scheduled.
performance_metric: QueueChannel buffered enqueue/dequeue ops/sec after parity

legacy_behavior: Main repeatedly receives from initially empty QueueChannel while a sender tasklet sends 0..9; result must be FIFO values 0..9 with final balance 0.
rust_state: The unchanged test_queuechannel.TestQueueChannels.test_blocking_receive_on_main_tasklet case passes against the Rust extension in the 210-test Python suite.
gap: No bridge gap remains for QueueChannel main receive drain; pure-core fixture coverage is deferred unless QueueChannel is migrated into core.
recommended_task: Keep this behavior covered through the unchanged legacy QueueChannel suite.

legacy_behavior: tearDown drains scheduler.run, calls unblock_all_channels, forces GC, asserts active channels are zero, then runs/GCs again before manager checks.
rust_state: The fixture runner now supports an opt-in teardown script with a separate post-teardown expected final state. fixture_teardown_blocked_channel_cleanup deliberately leaves one sender and one receiver blocked, runs a teardown drain/clear/drain sequence, and proves run_count=1, blocked_tasklet_count=0, blocked_channel_count=0, active_tasklet_count=1, and empty blocked sender/receiver queues. The scheduler fixture gate passes 55/55 fixtures and reports teardown_checked=true for that fixture.
gap: Pure core blocked-channel cleanup is covered. CPython active channel object lifetime, active schedule-manager refcount/GC checks, and full unblock_all_active_channels registry behavior remain FFI/core-registry work under SCH-UTILS-002 and SCH-CHANNEL-009.
recommended_task: Keep fixture-level teardown coverage in scheduler-fixtures, then add Python active-channel and active-manager lifetime counters in the bridge/CoreScheduler registry layer before full test_utils teardown parity.
performance_metric: cleanup latency per blocked channel

legacy_behavior: tearDown asserts sys.getrefcount(scheduler.get_schedule_manager()) == 2 and active schedule manager count == 1 after GC.
rust_state: Counters are planned; Python GC/refcount behavior is deferred.
gap: Core counters alone cannot prove CPython reference ownership for get_schedule_manager.
recommended_task: Add Python FFI refcount/lifetime test for get_schedule_manager and fixture final assertions for active schedule manager count.

legacy_behavior: getruncount helper asserts scheduler.getruncount() equals scheduler.calculateruncount() before returning.
rust_state: Runtime trace emission now records run_count and independently calculated_run_count on every scheduler event and final state. carbon-scheduler-trace rejects any event where the cached run count diverges from calculated_run_count or a runnable snapshot length.
gap: No current event-level run-count invariant gap remains for the core fixture corpus; Python/C API calculateruncount behavior remains covered by bridge evidence.
recommended_task: Keep the event-level cached-versus-calculated run-count invariant in scheduler-fixtures and extend it when new scheduler trace operations are added.
performance_metric: debug-only counter-check overhead

legacy_behavior: TestWithLimit loops while getruncount > 1 and calls scheduler.run_n_tasklets(1), exercising the same scenarios under bounded pumping.
rust_state: MainStep::RunSchedulerN drives count-limited scheduler pumping, and the fixture runner now supports trace_expect ordered partial event checks plus match counts. scheduler_run_n_tasklets_limited_nested_schedule_order and scheduler_run_n_tasklets_limited_schedule_order_no_nested cover single-level schedule-order TestWithLimit behavior for nested_tasklets true and false. scheduler_run_n_tasklets_limited_multi_level_nested_schedule_order and scheduler_run_n_tasklets_limited_multi_level_schedule_order_no_nested cover the multi-level schedule-order cases. The scheduler fixture gate passes 55/55 and each limited fixture records trace_expectations_checked=true with exact scheduler.run.start limit=1 counts.
gap: No pure-core TestWithLimit bounded-pumping fixture gap remains. Positive-time RunTaskletsForTime deadline policy remains under SCH-CORE-005.
recommended_task: Keep the limited schedule-order variants in scheduler-fixtures and add trace_expect checks to any future bounded pumping fixture that needs stop/resume proof without full event duplication.
performance_metric: dispatches/sec; p95 per-tasklet pump latency

legacy_behavior: TestNoNestedTasklets disables nested tasklets in setUp and tearDown restores true; base tearDown also restores true.
rust_state: Scenario-level nested_tasklets config exists and the fixture corpus includes nested=true and nested=false variants for targeted tasklet.run, nested run order, nested schedule order, multi-level nested schedule order, and blocked-yield scenarios. Each fixture constructs an isolated Runtime, so nested mode cannot leak between fixtures.
gap: No pure-core nested_tasklets fixture-mode gap remains for the current semantic corpus; Python global option API behavior remains covered by the Rust scheduler Python boundary evidence.
recommended_task: Keep nested_tasklets true/false variants in scheduler-fixtures and add new paired variants whenever nested scheduling semantics change.
performance_metric: completed tasklets/sec by nested_tasklets mode after both modes pass

The smoke blocks receiver tasklets, verifies PyChannel_GetBalance goes negative while a receiver is blocked, verifies PyChannel_GetQueue returns the blocked tasklet, wakes the receiver with PyChannel_Send, propagates RuntimeError through PyChannel_SendThrow with the channel balance returning to zero, and records the legacy Carbon IO trace comparison blocker.
